section.title: The Prophetic Word Confirmed
section.sectionRationale: Knowing his death is near, Peter explains why he keeps reminding the church of these truths, then anchors them in two unshakable witnesses: what he saw with his own eyes at the Transfiguration, and the prophetic word of Scripture, which came not from human will but from the Holy Spirit.
section.thesis: The gospel is no cleverly devised fable; Peter was an eyewitness of Christ's majesty, and the prophetic Scriptures, spoken by men carried along by the Holy Spirit, shine as a reliable lamp until Christ the morning star dawns.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Peter's farewell reminder, verses 12 to 15; the eyewitness of Christ's majesty, verses 16 to 18; and the prophetic word and its divine origin, verses 19 to 21.

verses.text: Therefore I will always remind you of these things, even though you know them and are established in the truth you now have.
I think it is right to refresh your memory as long as I live in the {{skenoma|tent of my body}},
because I know that this tent will soon be laid aside, as our Lord Jesus Christ has made clear to me.
And I will make every effort to ensure that after my {{exodos|departure}}, you will be able to recall these things at all times.

For we did not follow {{mythos|cleverly devised fables}} when we made known to you the power and {{parousia|coming}} of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were {{epoptes|eyewitnesses}} of His {{megaleiotes|majesty}}.
For He received honor and glory from God the Father when the voice came to Him from the Majestic Glory, saying, “This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.”
And we ourselves heard this voice from heaven when we were with Him on the holy mountain.

We also have the word of the prophets as confirmed beyond doubt. And you will do well to pay attention to it, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the {{phosphoros|morning star}} rises in your hearts.
Above all, you must understand that no prophecy of Scripture comes from {{epilysis|one’s own interpretation}}.
For no such prophecy was ever brought forth by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were {{pheromenoi|carried along}} by the Holy Spirit.

skenoma.gloss: A tent, dwelling, or lodging, used here for the body. Peter speaks of living in the tent of his body, a temporary shelter soon to be taken down. The image conveys that the body is a passing dwelling, while the person continues beyond death.

exodos.gloss: A departure, exit, or going out; the word behind Exodus. Peter calls his coming death his departure. The same word is used of Jesus at the Transfiguration in Luke 9:31, framing death for the believer as a purposeful going out, not mere extinction.

mythos.gloss: A myth, fable, or invented tale. Peter insists the apostles did not follow cleverly devised myths when they proclaimed Christ. The gospel is grounded in witnessed events, not in the fictions the false teachers would later spin.

parousia.gloss: A coming, arrival, or presence, the standard term for Christ's second coming. Peter proclaims the power and parousia of the Lord Jesus. The Transfiguration was a preview of the glory in which He will return.

epoptes.gloss: An eyewitness, one who has seen with his own eyes. Peter, James, and John were epoptai of Christ's majesty on the mountain. Their testimony rests on firsthand sight, not secondhand report, making the gospel a matter of history.

megaleiotes.gloss: Majesty, magnificence, greatness. At the Transfiguration the apostles beheld the megaleiotes of Christ, His divine glory shining through. It confirmed that Jesus is the beloved Son, honored and glorified by the Father.

phosphoros.gloss: The light-bringer, the morning star that heralds the dawn. Peter longs for this star to rise in his readers' hearts. It points to Christ Himself, whose full appearing will end the night and complete what the prophetic lamp now guides us through.

epilysis.gloss: A loosing, unraveling, or interpretation. Peter says no prophecy of Scripture came from one's own epilysis. Whether this means the prophet's own origination or the reader's private interpretation, the point is that Scripture's source is God, not human invention.

pheromenoi.gloss: Being carried, borne, or driven along, as a ship is carried by the wind. The prophets spoke as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit. God moved them so that their words are truly His, even as they remained active human authors.

basic.context: Peter knows he will die soon, so he explains why he keeps repeating these truths: he wants the church to remember them after he is gone. Then he gives two solid reasons to trust the gospel. First, he saw Jesus glorified with his own eyes on the mountain and heard God's voice call Him beloved Son. The message is not a made-up story. Second, the Scriptures of the prophets are even more sure, a lamp for a dark world, because they did not come from human imagination but from men moved by the Holy Spirit.
basic.hermeneutics: The great subject here is where Scripture comes from. Verses 20 to 21 teach that prophecy did not originate in the prophet's own mind or will; the prophets were carried along by the Holy Spirit, so their words are God's words. This is a foundational text for the inspiration of Scripture. Peter also links his eyewitness experience to the written word, and then says the written word is even more confirmed, so we test experience by Scripture, not the other way round.
basic.originalLanguages: Peter lives in the skenoma, the tent, of his body and awaits his exodos, his departure, like Jesus at the Transfiguration. The gospel is no mythos, no fable, for the apostles were epoptai, eyewitnesses, of Christ's megaleiotes, majesty, and His coming parousia. So we heed the prophetic lamp until the phosphoros, the morning star, rises. Scripture came from no human epilysis, for the prophets were pheromenoi, carried along, by the Holy Spirit.
basic.typology: The Transfiguration is a preview of Christ's second coming in glory, a mountain-top glimpse of the majesty in which He will return. Peter calling his death an exodos echoes the exodus from Egypt and Jesus' own departure, framing the believer's death as a passage to glory. And the morning star fulfills the ancient hope of a rising light, realized in Christ.
basic.christ: Christ is the majesty the apostles beheld, the beloved Son honored by the Father's voice, and the coming King whose glory the Transfiguration foreshadowed. He is the morning star whose dawning the whole prophetic word anticipates. Every line points to Him: seen in glory, declared by God, promised by the prophets, and awaited in His return.
basic.god: God the Father speaks from heaven, honoring His Son, and God the Holy Spirit carries the prophets so their words are truly divine. Here the Father, the Son, and the Spirit are all at work. God is the ultimate author of Scripture, the one whose voice validated Christ and whose Spirit inspired the prophets.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 17:1-8 records the Transfiguration Peter witnessed. Second Timothy 3:16 says all Scripture is God-breathed. Luke 9:31 calls Jesus' coming death His exodus, the word Peter uses. And Revelation 22:16 names Jesus the bright morning star.
basic.oneStory: God spoke through the prophets, carried by His Spirit, and then in the fullness of time revealed His Son in majesty, witnessed by Peter on the mountain. The written word and the seen glory agree, both pointing to Christ, the morning star whose full dawning the church awaits. Scripture is the reliable lamp lighting the whole story until He appears.

context.literary: The passage pivots from exhortation to testimony. Peter frames it as a farewell, verses 12 to 15, giving the letter the weight of last words. Then he offers two grounds for confidence, personal eyewitness, verses 16 to 18, and the prophetic Scriptures, verses 19 to 21, moving from what he saw to what is written, and ranking the written word as even more sure.
context.historical: Peter writes near his death, which Jesus had foretold, John 21:18-19, likely from Rome in the mid 60s. False teachers dismissed the promise of Christ's coming as myth. Peter counters with his firsthand memory of the Transfiguration and with the settled authority of the Hebrew Scriptures his readers already held.
context.canonical: Verses 20 to 21 are a cornerstone text for the doctrine of Scripture, alongside 2 Timothy 3:16. The Transfiguration links this passage to the Gospel accounts, and the morning star ties it to the Bible's expectation of a coming light, fulfilled in Christ and consummated in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To leave the church a lasting reminder before Peter's death, and to establish the trustworthiness of the gospel on two grounds: apostolic eyewitness of Christ's majesty and the divinely inspired prophetic Scriptures.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 12 to 18 describe Peter's situation and testimony; verses 19 to 21 both describe Scripture's origin and prescribe a response, pay attention to the prophetic word as to a lamp. The abiding call is to heed the God-given Scriptures until Christ appears.

debates.question: What does no prophecy of Scripture comes from one's own interpretation mean, verse 20?

views.label: It concerns the origin of prophecy, not private reading
views.case: Verse 21 explains verse 20: prophecy was not brought forth by human will but by the Spirit. So one's own interpretation means the prophet's own origination or unraveling of events; the prophets did not invent their message. This reading fits the flow of thought and the doctrine of inspiration.

views.label: It warns against private, individualistic interpretation
views.case: Others read it as a caution to readers: Scripture is not to be interpreted by isolated private opinion, apart from the Spirit and the church. This guards against the false teachers twisting the text, as 3:16 warns.

debates.bearing: The stronger reading, supported by verse 21, is that Peter is teaching the divine origin of prophecy: it came from God, not human will. Yet both readings agree Scripture is God's word and cannot be bent to serve human agendas, which is exactly Peter's concern against the false teachers.

debates.question: How does verse 21 describe the way the Spirit inspired the prophets?

views.label: The prophets were genuinely carried along while remaining real authors
views.case: The word pheromenoi pictures a ship carried by the wind. God so moved the prophets that their words are His words, yet they spoke as active human authors with their own styles. This is the historic doctrine of verbal, plenary inspiration: fully divine and fully human.

views.label: A dictation model where the human role is minimal
views.case: A minority have read inspiration as near-dictation, the human author a passive pen. While it rightly stresses divine authorship, it underplays the evident personality and craft of the biblical writers that Scripture itself displays.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading holds both truths together: Scripture is entirely from God, carried by the Spirit, and genuinely written by human authors. This grounds our confidence that the Bible is trustworthy and authoritative as the very word of God.

study.originalLanguages: Peter writes as one whose days are numbered, dwelling in the skenoma, the tent, of his body and awaiting his exodos, his departure, the very word Luke uses of Jesus' death at the Transfiguration. The apostolic message is no mythos, no cleverly devised fable, for Peter and his companions were epoptai, eyewitnesses, of Christ's megaleiotes, His majesty, a foretaste of His parousia, His coming. Therefore the church heeds the prophetic lamp until the phosphoros, the morning star, rises in the heart. And that word is utterly reliable, for no prophecy sprang from human epilysis; the prophets were pheromenoi, carried along, by the Holy Spirit, so that their words are God's own.

typology.type: The Transfiguration on the holy mountain
typology.fulfillment: A preview of Christ's second coming in majesty, the glory in which the beloved Son will return, Matthew 17 and 2 Peter 1:16.

typology.type: Peter's exodos, his departure in death
typology.fulfillment: Echoes the exodus and Jesus' own departure, framing the believer's death as a passage out to glory rather than an end, Luke 9:31.
The rising light long awaited by the prophets, fulfilled in Christ, the bright morning star, Revelation 22:16.

study.christ: Christ fills this passage with His glory. On the holy mountain the apostles beheld His majesty and heard the Father declare, This is My beloved Son. That vision was a preview of His power and coming, the parousia the false teachers denied. He is the goal of all prophecy, the morning star whose dawning the prophetic lamp anticipates and whose full appearing will flood the world with day. To trust the gospel is to trust the witnessed, God-declared, prophesied Christ, seen in glory and coming again.
study.god: The whole Trinity is at work here. God the Father speaks from the Majestic Glory, honoring His Son with a voice from heaven. God the Son is revealed in divine majesty. God the Holy Spirit carries the prophets so that their words are truly God's word. Above all, God is shown as the author of Scripture: prophecy did not arise from human will but from His Spirit. He is a God who speaks, who validates His Son, and who has given a written word that can be utterly trusted.

crossReferences.note: The Transfiguration Peter witnessed, where the Father's voice declared Jesus His beloved Son, the event behind verses 16 to 18.
All Scripture is God-breathed, the companion text to Peter's teaching that the prophets were carried by the Spirit.
At the Transfiguration Moses and Elijah spoke of Jesus' departure, His exodus, the same word Peter uses of his own death.
Jesus is the bright morning star, the light Peter longs to see rise in his readers' hearts.

study.oneStory: God has always spoken, moving the prophets by His Spirit so their words were His own, and then in the fullness of time He unveiled His Son in majesty on the mountain, witnessed by Peter. The seen glory and the written word agree, both testifying to Christ. The prophetic Scriptures are the trustworthy lamp that lights the church through the dark until the morning star, Christ Himself, rises in full day. This is the reliable thread running through the whole biblical story to its dawn in Him.

sermon.title: An Eyewitness Account
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in 2 Peter, this message treats 2 Peter 1:12-21 directly, Peter's farewell reminder, the eyewitness testimony of the Transfiguration, and the divine origin of the prophetic word. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: Why does Peter keep reminding the church of these truths?

options.text: Because he knows he will die soon and wants them to remember after his departure.
options.note: Verses 13 to 15: he writes as a farewell, so the truths will endure past his death.

options.text: Because he doubts whether the truths are actually reliable.
options.note: The opposite: he is utterly confident, grounding the truths in eyewitness and Scripture.

options.text: Because the readers had never heard these things before.
options.note: They already know them and are established; he reminds rather than introduces.

options.text: Because he wants them established and unshaken in the truth.
options.note: True and part of his aim, though the stated reason is his approaching death.

quiz.q: What does Peter appeal to as proof that the gospel is not a myth?

options.text: He was an eyewitness of Christ's majesty and heard God's voice at the Transfiguration.
options.note: Verses 16 to 18: firsthand sight and the Father's voice, not invented tales.

options.text: The cleverness of the arguments the apostles devised.
options.note: He explicitly denies following cleverly devised fables.

options.text: That he was present with Jesus on the holy mountain.
options.note: True, that is the setting, though the point is his eyewitness of Christ's majesty.

options.text: The wide popularity of the Christian message.
options.note: Peter grounds truth in witnessed events, not popularity.

quiz.q: According to verses 20 to 21, where did the prophetic Scriptures come from?

options.text: From God, as men spoke while carried along by the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Verse 21: not the will of man, but the Spirit moving the prophets.

options.text: From the prophets' own insight and imagination.
options.note: Peter expressly denies that prophecy came by the will of man.

options.text: From men moved by the Spirit while still writing as real authors.
options.note: True to the doctrine of inspiration, both divine and human, though it should name God as the source.

options.text: From the collected wisdom of Israel's traditions.
options.note: The source is the Holy Spirit, not merely human tradition.

quiz.q: How does Peter rank the prophetic word alongside his own eyewitness experience?

options.text: The written prophetic word is fully confirmed and to be heeded as a lamp until Christ dawns.
options.note: Verse 19: Scripture is the sure lamp; experience is tested by the word, not above it.

options.text: Personal experience always outranks the written word.
options.note: Peter points to the prophetic word as even more sure than his vision.

options.text: The two agree, both testifying to Christ.
options.note: True, they agree, though Peter's stress falls on the reliability of the written word.

options.text: The prophetic word is useful only until we have our own experiences.
options.note: The lamp shines until Christ Himself appears, not until private experience replaces it.

quiz.q: What is the morning star Peter longs to see rise in his readers' hearts?

options.text: Christ Himself, whose full appearing ends the night the prophetic lamp guides us through.
options.note: Verse 19 points to Christ, the bright morning star of Revelation 22:16.

options.text: A literal star that will appear in the sky.
options.note: The image is of Christ's dawning, not a mere astronomical event.

options.text: The dawning light of Christ growing bright within believers.
options.note: True to the image, though it is finally Christ Himself who rises.

options.text: The believer's own inner enlightenment apart from Christ.
options.note: The light is Christ, not a self-generated illumination.

questions: Peter calls his coming death a departure and his body a tent. How does viewing the body as a temporary dwelling change the way you face aging, illness, or death?
The gospel rests on eyewitness testimony, not clever myths. How does the historical, witnessed nature of the faith strengthen your confidence in it?
Peter treats the written Scriptures as even more sure than his own vision of glory. Where are you tempted to trust your experiences over God's word, and how might you reverse that?
The prophets were carried along by the Holy Spirit. How does believing that the Bible is truly God's word shape the way you read and obey it?
Peter longs for the morning star to rise in his readers' hearts. What would it look like for you to live today in eager hope of Christ's appearing?

translationNotes.note: The tent of my body renders skenoma; a footnote notes the literal as long as I am in this tent, picturing the body as a temporary dwelling.
Departure translates exodos, the same word used of Jesus' death at the Transfiguration in Luke 9:31.
The Father's words echo Matthew 17:5; see also Mark 9:7 and Luke 9:35, the voice at the Transfiguration.
One's own interpretation renders epilysis; in context, clarified by verse 21, it concerns the divine origin of prophecy rather than private reading, though it also guards against twisting the text.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Peter 1:12-21. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
